title: Crew Missing After Cargo Ship Capsizes in Super Typhoon
summary: A U.S.-registered cargo ship named the Mariana overturned near the Northern Mariana Islands during Super Typhoon Sinlaku, one of the strongest storms this year. The ship had six crew members. Searchers have found the body of one crew member and are still looking for the other five. The U.S. Coast Guard and Japanese divers used an underwater drone to search inside the overturned ship but did not find the missing crew. They are also searching for an orange life raft that might have been deployed. The search covers an area of over 99,000 square miles, about the size of Oregon. The ship lost its starboard engine during the typhoon and contacted the Coast Guard for help on April 15, but communication was lost the next day. The storm had sustained winds of up to 150 mph. The Coast Guard has expressed sympathy for the families and is continuing the search with help from agencies in Guam, Japan, and New Zealand.

background_read: The Northern Mariana Islands are a U.S. territory in the Pacific. Typhoons are common in this region. Cargo ships must have life rafts that can withstand 30 days at sea. The International Maritime Organization sets safety codes for ships.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The article reports on the search for five missing crew members after their cargo ship capsized during Super Typhoon Sinlaku. One body has been found. The search involves multiple agencies and covers a vast area. Purpose: To inform the public about a maritime disaster and ongoing rescue efforts. Evidence Evaluation: The article relies on official statements from the U.S. Coast Guard and other agencies, which are credible sources. However, details about the crew's fate remain unknown. Author Credibility: The Associated Press is a reputable news agency. Methodology: Information was gathered from press releases and official reports. The article does not include independent verification.
